era_context: A pivotal year marking the transition from Ashur-dan II to Ashurnasirpal II in Assyria. The early Iron Age II continues in the Levant and Mediterranean. Egypt remains fragmented. Greece is in the Geometric period. The Zhou Dynasty in China is in its middle period.

events.title: Death of Ashur-dan II and accession of Ashurnasirpal II to the Assyrian throne
events.description: Ashur-dan II dies after a 22-year reign (934–912 BCE) and is succeeded by his son Ashurnasirpal II (r. 912–891 BCE). Ashurnasirpal II will become one of the most famous and feared Assyrian kings, known for his military campaigns, administrative reforms, and the construction of a new capital at Kalhu (Nimrud). His accession marks the beginning of a period of dramatic Assyrian expansion and the consolidation of the Neo-Assyrian Empire's military and administrative apparatus.

sources.name: Ashurnasirpal II's royal inscriptions and annals

events.certainty_note: The accession of Ashurnasirpal II in 912 BCE is well-established from king lists and his own inscriptions. The date is secure.

disconfirming_evidence: No disconfirming evidence identified for the accession itself. However, some older scholarship dated Ashurnasirpal II's reign to 883–859 BCE (using different chronological systems); modern scholarship has settled on 912–891 BCE based on synchronisms with Egyptian and Babylonian records.
historiographic_note: The transition of power in Assyria is well-documented through king lists and royal inscriptions. However, the record is heavily focused on political and military succession; the social, economic, and cultural dimensions of the transition are poorly documented. The sources are Assyrian court records, which naturally emphasize the legitimacy and achievements of the new king.

graph_edges.note: Ashurnasirpal II's accession initiated a period of intensive military campaigns beginning in his first regnal year.
